# freeday — Design Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 16:46:57._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: freeday

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Freeday

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **5**.

## App Name

Freeday

## Category

Habit Quitting / Craving Tracker

## One-Sentence Promise

Track any habit you're quitting — cigarettes, vaping, sugar, doomscrolling — and watch your clean streak and money saved grow, one Freeday at a time.

## Target User

The enormous population trying to quit or cut back on something — smokers, vapers, people cutting sugar or alcohol, people breaking a doomscrolling habit — a mass-market need spanning age and background.

## Painful Moment Solved

Wanting to quit something and having only single-purpose apps that don't talk to each other, plus feeling alone in the moment of a craving with no quick, non-judgmental tool to ride it out.

## Why It Is Different

One flexible engine works for any habit instead of a dozen single-purpose cessation apps — name your habit, log a cost-per-use, and Freeday tracks streak and running savings, with a 90-second guided 'ride the craving' moment when tempted, ending in a shareable milestone card ('47 days, $310 saved') tuned for social sharing rather than clinical tracking.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers one habit, streak tracking, and milestone cards; Freeday+ unlocks unlimited simultaneous habits, the full craving-support toolkit, and a Lock Screen craving-timer Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr — positioned as cheaper than one week of the habit itself, stated explicitly in the paywall copy.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData store for habits, relapse/reset events, and craving-session logs; savings math computed on-device; all guided craving-support content bundled locally, no network dependency.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows CloudKit sync later; an opt-in anonymous community layer is a plausible v2 but explicitly out of scope for v1 to avoid backend/moderation overhead.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None; explicitly not using LLM chat-based coaching — the craving-support flow is a fixed, well-designed guided sequence that must work instantly and offline.

## Design Direction

Clean, encouraging, and light — soft mint-to-sky-blue gradient system, rounded friendly iconography, a sunrise/counter motif for 'days free,' deliberately non-clinical, non-shaming tone.

## App Store Positioning

One app for quitting anything — not just cigarettes. Track your streak, watch your savings grow, and get through the next craving without white-knuckling it alone.

## Invalidation Criteria

If most users who log a relapse never open the app again within a week, the reset/relapse flow is driving shame-based abandonment and needs a redesign before any other feature work.

## v1 Build Scope

Habit setup with cost-per-use, daily streak tracking with non-punitive reset, craving-timer guided flow, milestone recap card + ShareLink, widget, Freeday+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- set up a habit with cost-per-use
- daily check-in / streak tracking with one-tap non-punitive relapse-and-restart
- in-the-moment craving timer with guided ride-it-out flow
- milestone streak + savings recap card
- manage multiple concurrent habits (paid tier)

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for an active craving-timer session
- WidgetKit showing current streak + savings
- ShareLink for milestone card
- haptics for streak-day and craving-timer completion

## Key Risks

- Relapse handling is the make-or-break UX moment — punitive 'streak broken, back to zero' framing will cause uninstalls; needs a genuinely non-shaming reset flow with a preserved lifetime-total stat from day one
- This category brushes against health claims — copy must stay in the 'personal tracking tool' lane, not present as medical/addiction treatment advice

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design an encouraging habit-quitting app called Freeday: soft mint-to-sky-blue gradient system, rounded friendly iconography, a sunrise/counter motif representing 'days free,' warm and non-judgmental tone throughout, avoid clinical white/red or guilt-inducing imagery, a milestone streak+savings card as the hero shareable artifact, calm and supportive rather than gamified-aggressive.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide UX flows, information architecture, key screens/states, and the design principles that govern them.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Let me put myself in the room as the person who has to defend a specific user against a confusing or joyless screen, and lay out the actual screens this app needs.

Picture someone who just white-knuckled through a Tuesday night vape craving, opened the app, and now it's Wednesday morning — that's who I'm designing the home screen for, not a hypothetical "user." So here's my screen inventory, and I want to be strict about keeping it small: Launch/Splash (instant, sub-second, no logo animation that makes anyone wait), Onboarding-Welcome (one screen, states the promise, one button, no swipeable carousel — carousels are where people bail), Habit Setup (a single screen with progressive disclosure, not three separate screens: name field up top, then a "how much does this cost you?" section that's visually secondary and has a visible "skip" that doesn't feel like a dead end, cost-per-use and uses-per-day appear together as a linked pair only if the user taps into that section at all), Home/Streak (the screen people live in — day count as the hero number, optional savings beneath it in a smaller weight, the craving button as a large persistent element that never scrolls out of view, and a very quiet reset affordance that is deliberately not styled like a danger button), Craving Timer/Guided Flow (full-screen takeover, no chrome, no back button that tempts an early exit, a monotonic countdown, calming full-bleed gradient animation), Craving Complete (not a separate navigational destination — it's a state transition back into Home with a brief reinforcement moment, more on that below), Milestone Card / Share Sheet (a modal or sheet presentation over Home, rendered as an image preview plus the native ShareLink), Habit Detail/Edit (reachable from a small settings affordance, lets you rename, edit cost, or delete), Multi-Habit List (paid tier only, a plain list — I want to be honest that this is not a place to spend design budget, it's a list with a "+" that either navigates to Habit Setup or hits the paywall), Paywall (presented as a sheet, triggered specifically at the moment of adding habit #2, never at first launch), Settings/About (disclaimer lives here, plus habit management entry point).

The primary flow I care about most isn't setup, it's the two loops layered on top of each other. Loop one, the ambient one: open app, home screen renders from cached SwiftData state before any spinner could even show, user sees their number, closes the app. That's it — if this ever shows a loading spinner on a warm launch, that's a bug, not a state. Loop two, the acute one: home screen, one tap on the craving button, and the guided flow needs to already be visually "arriving" within a frame or two — I'd push back hard on any transition here fancier than a fast, springy full-screen cover, because a slow modal presentation animation is friction at the exact moment friction is most dangerous. The guided flow runs its script, and when it completes, I do not want a "Congratulations!" screen with confetti and a tap-to-continue button — that's an extra screen standing between someone and being done, and it also risks feeling gamified-aggressive, which the spec explicitly said to avoid. Instead the timer's final frame settles, a haptic fires, and the view auto-dismisses back to Home half a second later with a small transient reinforcement (a soft highlight or checkmark that fades on the craving button itself, not a new screen). One tap in, zero taps out.

Reset is the flow I'd protect most jealously in this whole app, and I want to describe its actual mechanical states because "non-punitive" is a slogan until it's a specific state machine. Tap reset: no confirm sheet, no alert. Home screen instantly re-renders as if day 1 already happened — day count flips to "Day 1," lifetime-total (a smaller, quieter stat that was already on screen, not one that appears for the first time now) stays exactly where it was. Simultaneously a snackbar slides up from the bottom edge, low-contrast, reading "Day 1 · Undo," auto-dismissing in 5 seconds. If Undo is tapped, the home screen reverts and nothing was ever persisted — visually this needs to look like a value flicker, not a screen change, or it'll read as "undoing a mistake" rather than "changing your mind," which are different emotional registers. My one real worry about my own design here: if the reset affordance is too subtle, someone in a moment of self-directed frustration might not be able to find it, or might tap it repeatedly looking for a confirmation that never comes and wonder if it worked — I think the fix is that the day-count number itself needs a very brief, satisfying transition (a soft cross-fade/count change, not a jarring hard-cut) so the UI visibly acknowledges the tap without needing a dialog to do it.

State model, screen by screen, the parts that actually determine what gets built: Home has empty (first-run, no habit — this isn't really "empty," it should redirect straight into setup, there's no reason to ever show a blank home screen), loading (should basically never be visible given local SwiftData, but exists for the first frame after a fresh install before the store initializes), and populated — and populated itself branches on whether cost is set (streak+savings vs. streak-only, and I want to be explicit that streak-only is not a degraded state visually, it's a fully intentional card layout, same visual weight, just one fewer row). Habit Setup has the two-tier form state (name-only vs. name-with-cost-expanded) and a validation error state limited to the cost sanity ceiling (an inline warning, not a blocking alert, when someone fat-fingers $5000). Craving Timer has running, paused-by-interruption (phone call, backgrounding — resumes on return using elapsed monotonic time, doesn't restart), and abandoned (user force-exits or force-quits — logged honestly, and if they reopen the app there's no lingering "resume your session?" prompt hanging over Home, that would just be a guilt reminder wearing a helpful hat). Milestone Card has rendering (should be near-instant since it's local composition, but needs a skeleton state for the split second before the image is composed) and a rendered-with-no-cost variant that swaps the dollar line for something equally confident, not a blank gap — I'd suggest the card leads with the day count large regardless of cost, so the no-cost version doesn't read as "the cheap version of the real card." Paywall has default (feature comparison, pricing) and a purchase-in-flight state (native StoreKit sheet takes over, nothing custom to build there). Settings has the plain static disclaimer text, always visible, never a modal to dismiss.

On visual direction: I want the mint-to-sky-blue gradient system doing real work, not just sitting as a header decoration — I'd use it as the background field on Home and the Craving Timer specifically, because those are the two screens where the emotional tone matters most, and I'd keep Habit Setup and Settings on a plain, calmer neutral background so the gradient reads as special rather than wallpaper everywhere. The sunrise/counter motif is the natural fit for the day-count number itself — I'd treat the number as the actual sunrise, maybe a subtle radial glow behind the digit that very slowly brightens as the streak grows, rather than a literal illustrated sun icon competing for attention next to the number. Iconography rounded and soft throughout, and I want to explicitly rule out any color-coded red/orange "danger" treatment anywhere near the reset control — if reset gets colored like a destructive action anywhere in this app, we've silently reintroduced the shame framing through color alone, independent of what the copy says.

Accessibility: the reset VoiceOver announcement needs to say "Day 1" exactly like a normal daily VoiceOver read of the home screen would, per what detailed_discussion already locked — I want that same discipline applied to the craving-complete transition too, so VoiceOver announces something like "Day continues" or just re-reads the day count, not "session complete" in a tone that implies a test was passed or failed. Every tappable target — craving button, reset, share, skip — needs to hit 44pt minimum, and I'd flag the reset affordance specifically as a place designers will be tempted to shrink it down out of "let's not make it prominent," which is the right instinct visually but can't come at the cost of it being a legitimate, reachable tap target. Dynamic Type needs real testing on the Home screen specifically, since the day-count number wants to be huge for impact, and huge-number-plus-largest-accessibility-text-size is exactly the combination that breaks layouts — I'd rather the number scale down gracefully than the screen require horizontal scrolling.

The single biggest risk to my own plan: collapsing habit setup into one progressive-disclosure screen instead of three separate screens saves taps, which I think is right for the under-60-seconds goal, but if it's built sloppily it can look like one cluttered form rather than an airy, calm first impression — which would undercut the entire "clean, encouraging, light" design direction before the user even gets to day 1. Worth a second look once real spacing is in front of us rather than something I'd insist on defending to the death right now.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

Only one voice showed up this round (Codex still on cooldown), but the pass is genuinely complete — it delivers all five things the phase playbook requires (screen inventory, primary flow, state model, visual direction, accessibility notes) and every call it makes is a direct, careful execution of what got locked in the five prior phases rather than a re-litigation of them. The reset-flow state machine, the "craving complete is a state transition not a screen," the habit-setup-as-one-progressive-disclosure-screen call, and the explicit ban on red/orange near the reset control are all concrete, buildable decisions that operationalize the non-punitive-reset requirement instead of just restating it. Nothing here conflicts with prompt_contract, detailed_discussion, or app_features. The one thing flagged as worth a second look — whether the collapsed one-screen habit setup stays airy once real spacing is in front of us — is named as a build-time judgment call, not an open product disagreement, so it doesn't block calling consensus now.

## Final Output

**Screen inventory**: Launch/Splash (instant, no animation) · Onboarding-Welcome (single screen, one button, no carousel) · Habit Setup (one screen, progressive disclosure — name on top, cost/uses-per-day pair revealed only if tapped into, visible non-dead-end skip) · Home/Streak (day count hero, optional savings subordinate, persistent craving button, quiet non-danger-styled reset) · Craving Timer/Guided Flow (full-screen takeover, no chrome, monotonic countdown, calming gradient) · Craving Complete (not a separate screen — a state transition back into Home with a transient haptic/highlight reinforcement) · Milestone Card / Share Sheet (modal/sheet over Home, image preview + native ShareLink) · Habit Detail/Edit (rename, edit cost, delete) · Multi-Habit List (paid tier, plain list, "+" routes to setup or paywall) · Paywall (sheet, triggered specifically at habit #2, never at first launch) · Settings/About (disclaimer, habit management entry).

**Primary user flow**: Two loops stacked on the same home screen. Ambient loop: open app → home renders instantly from cached SwiftData (no spinner on warm launch) → close. Acute loop: home → one tap on the craving button → guided flow arrives in a frame or two via a fast springy full-screen cover → script runs on monotonic time → on completion, haptic fires and the view auto-dismisses back to Home ~0.5s later with a small transient reinforcement on the craving button itself — zero taps out, no congratulations screen. Reset flow, specified as an actual mechanism: tap reset → no confirm dialog → Home instantly re-renders as Day 1 with the lifetime-total stat staying exactly where it was → a low-contrast "Day 1 · Undo" snackbar slides up, auto-dismissing in 5 seconds → tapping Undo reverts the home screen as a value flicker (nothing was ever persisted), not a screen change.

**State model per screen**: Home — first-run empty state routes straight into Setup rather than showing a blank screen; a brief loading state exists only for the first frame post-install; populated state branches on cost-set vs. cost-unset, with streak-only treated as a fully intentional, equal-weight layout, not a degraded one. Habit Setup — name-only vs. name-with-cost-expanded, plus an inline (non-blocking) validation warning at the cost sanity ceiling. Craving Timer — running, paused-by-interruption (resumes via elapsed monotonic time, no restart), abandoned (logged honestly, no lingering "resume?" prompt on reopen). Milestone Card — a brief render/skeleton state before the local image composes, plus a no-cost variant that leads with the day count rather than showing a blank gap where a dollar figure would be. Paywall — default comparison/pricing view and a purchase-in-flight state handed off entirely to the native StoreKit sheet. Settings — static, always-visible disclaimer, no dismiss-me modal.

**Visual direction**: Mint-to-sky-blue gradient reserved for Home and the Craving Timer only (the two screens where tone matters most), so it reads as special rather than wallpaper; Setup and Settings sit on a calmer neutral background. The sunrise/counter motif lives in the day-count number itself — a subtle radial glow behind the digit that slowly brightens with streak length — rather than a separate illustrated sun icon. Iconography stays rounded and soft throughout. Hard rule: no red/orange or any destructive-action color treatment anywhere near the reset control, since that would silently reintroduce shame framing through color even with correct copy.

**Accessibility notes**: VoiceOver on reset announces "Day 1" exactly as a normal daily read would (per detailed_discussion), and the same discipline extends to craving-complete, which should re-read the day count rather than announce anything that implies pass/fail. All tappable targets (craving button, reset, share, skip) hit 44pt minimum, with reset specifically flagged as a target designers will be tempted to shrink into unreachability while making it visually quiet. Dynamic Type gets real testing on Home specifically — the large day-count number should scale down gracefully rather than force horizontal scrolling at the largest accessibility text sizes.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This clears the bar. All five required outputs are present and concrete:

- **Primary user flow**: Both loops (ambient and acute) are fully sequenced, and the reset mechanism is specified as an actual state machine (tap → instant re-render → snackbar → undo-as-flicker), not restated slogan language — this is exactly the kind of concreteness detailed_discussion and app_features demanded.
- **State model per screen**: Covers empty/loading/success branches per the global rubric (Home's first-run redirect instead of blank state, cost-set vs. cost-unset as equal-weight variants, craving timer's running/paused/abandoned trio, milestone card's skeleton + no-cost variant). Error states are thin (only the cost-ceiling inline warning is named), but that's consistent with this being a mostly-offline, mostly-input-free app — there isn't much surface for error states beyond what's covered.
- **Visual direction**: Makes deliberate, non-generic calls (gradient reserved for two emotionally-loaded screens only, sunrise motif folded into the digit itself rather than a separate icon, explicit red/orange ban near reset) tied directly to the spec's "non-clinical, non-shaming" mandate.
- **Accessibility notes**: Extends the already-locked VoiceOver "Day 1" rule to the craving-complete transition, sets 44pt targets with a specific callout on the reset-button shrink risk, and flags Dynamic Type scaling on the hero number.

Nothing contradicts prompt_contract, detailed_discussion, or app_features — the reset flow, non-medical disclaimer placement, cost-optional card treatment, and paywall trigger point (habit #2, not first launch) are all consistent continuations. The single-voice round is acceptable under this batch's time-pressure rule since the output is substantive and self-critiques its one open judgment call (habit-setup screen density) rather than hiding it. Acceptance checks satisfied: a SwiftUI developer has a buildable view hierarchy, and every required workflow (setup, streak, reset, craving timer, milestone share, paywall) has an explicit screen/state path. Ready to hand off to the build phase.

title: Design Handoff
output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Same pattern as every prior phase on this app — Codex is still in cooldown, so it's a single voice, but this pass earns consensus on its own merits rather than needing a second opinion to pad it out. The Frontend Engineer took the already-locked screen inventory and interaction model from design_discussion and turned it into something an engineer could actually start typing: a concrete navigation architecture (one `NavigationStack` rooted at Home, everything else as `.fullScreenCover`/`.sheet` off an enum-based state, explicitly banning swipe-to-dismiss on the craving timer so it can't accidentally read as "abandon session"), a real data-flow answer for the widget/Live Activity cross-process problem that nobody had spelled out yet (App Group shared snapshot, written atomically alongside every SwiftData mutation), concrete `ContinuousClock`-based timer mechanics that make the monotonic-time requirement from detailed_discussion actually buildable, hard token values (hex colors, spacing scale, corner radii, type choices), and a named, reusable component inventory. It also makes an explicit call on the one open process question — no external Claude Design needed to start the build, with a narrowly-scoped optional prompt offered only for the milestone card's polish pass later. Nothing here contradicts prompt_contract, detailed_discussion, app_features, or design_discussion; it's a direct, faithful translation of those decisions into implementation-ready specifics. No live disagreement to hash out.

## Final Output

**Screen-by-screen design specification** (extending design_discussion's inventory with implementation-level detail):

- **Home** — root of the single `NavigationStack`. Full-bleed `LinearGradient` (mint→sky, ~135°) with a very slow (~60s) hue drift via `TimelineView(.animation)`. Top-to-bottom: secondary habit-name label (tappable → Habit Detail push) · day-count hero number (SF Pro Rounded black, ~96–120pt scaled by Dynamic Type, `.minimumScaleFactor` rather than wrap) with a radial glow behind it whose intensity scales with `log(streakDays)` · savings row beneath in smaller secondary weight, *fully absent from layout* (not hidden) when cost is unset · generous negative space · craving button (coral `#FF9E80` pill/circle, ≥120pt) as the one high-saturation actionable element · quiet text-only reset control at the bottom, no fill/border, low-contrast gray, real 44×44pt tap target via padding around a visually small glyph.
- **Craving Timer** — presented via `.fullScreenCover`, no chrome, no swipe-to-dismiss (only a small explicit "I'm okay now" exit-early control, top corner, that logs the session as `abandoned`). Cooler/more-desaturated gradient variant, circular progress ring driven by `ContinuousClock` elapsed/duration ratio, monospaced-digit countdown centered in the ring, guidance script cross-fading beneath with no manual advance controls.
- **Craving Complete** — not a screen; auto-dismiss back to Home ~0.5s after completion with haptic + a one-shot `.scaleEffect` pulse/color highlight on the craving button.
- **Milestone Card / Share Sheet** — a standalone exportable view (not a Home screenshot), fixed portrait canvas (~1080×1350), rendered via `ImageRenderer`, handed to `ShareLink`. Habit-name/wordmark small at top, day count enormous and centered, savings beneath if set, thin sunrise-glow motif, small wordmark bottom corner so reshares are recognizable out of context.
- **Habit Setup, Multi-Habit List, Paywall, Settings/About, Habit Detail/Edit** — as scoped in design_discussion; setup is one progressive-disclosure screen (name → optional cost/uses-per-day pair on tap-in), Multi-Habit List is a plain list (not a design-budget screen), Paywall is a sheet triggered only at habit #2, Settings carries the static always-visible disclaimer.

**Design tokens**: gradient stops mint `#A8E6CF` → sky `#7FC7E8`; accent/craving-button coral `#FF9E80` (explicitly not red/orange); neutral background `#F7F9FA` light / `#111417` dark; primary text `#1C2B2A` light / `#EAF4F2` dark, secondary at 60% opacity; corner radii 12/16/24; spacing scale 4/8/16/24/32/48; SF Pro Rounded for numerals/headlines, standard SF Pro for body.

**Component inventory**: `GradientBackground(variant:)`, `DayCounterHero`, `SavingsRow` (returns `EmptyView` internals when nil), `CravingButton`, `QuietResetControl`, `UndoSnackbar`, `CravingProgressRing`, `MilestoneCardView`, `HabitSetupCard`, `PaywallFeatureRow`, `SettingsDisclaimerBlock`.

**State/interaction notes**: Single `@Observable AppState` enum-driven presentation layer (`.none/.onboarding/.cravingTimer(Habit)/.milestoneCard(Habit)/.paywall`); a single `@Observable HabitStore` service owns the `ModelContext` and is the sole source of streak/savings values (no view runs its own `@Query` for streak math); day-boundary math centralized in one injectable-`Date`-provider `StreakCalculator` in a shared package usable by app, widget, and Live Activity targets; widget/Live Activity share data via an App Group snapshot written atomically alongside every SwiftData mutation, paired with a synchronous `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadAllTimelines()` call; craving timer uses `ContinuousClock`/`Instant`-based elapsed time (never `Date`-vs-`Timer`), recomputing elapsed on resume rather than resuming a paused counter. Reset uses `.contentTransition(.numericText())` for a cross-fade "value updated" feel; snackbar slides in with a no-overshoot spring.

**Accessibility requirements**: Dynamic Type tested at `.accessibility3`+ on Home with the hero number and craving button independently scale-limited so the button never drops below 44pt; VoiceOver reading order habit name → day count → savings → craving button → reset; reset gets an explicit label/hint ("Start over — today becomes day one") despite its visually quiet styling; contrast-checked coral button text and glow-vs-hero-number contrast at high Dynamic Type sizes.

**Claude Design decision**: Not needed to start the SwiftUI build — four phases of design work already provide a complete screen inventory, a specified interaction state machine, concrete tokens, and named components. Optional, non-blocking exception: an external polish pass on the Milestone Card only, since it's the entire viral artifact. If pursued, prompt: *"Design a shareable milestone card for a habit-quitting app called Freeday, portrait aspect ratio for social sharing, soft mint-to-sky-blue gradient background, a huge centered day-count number with a subtle warm radial glow behind it, an optional savings-amount line beneath in a smaller weight, small wordmark bottom corner, rounded friendly type, calm and proud tone rather than gamified or trophy-like, must look complete and intentional even when the savings line is absent."*

**Named build-order risk carried forward**: the shared streak-math package and App Group snapshot-writing must be built deliberately as real cross-process infrastructure, not assumed to "just work" from calling the same function in two targets — a stale/wrong widget value is only catchable by actually installing the widget, not by running the simulator alone.

# freeday — Design Handoff

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 16:48:33._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: freeday

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Freeday

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **5**.

## App Name

Freeday

## Category

Habit Quitting / Craving Tracker

## One-Sentence Promise

Track any habit you're quitting — cigarettes, vaping, sugar, doomscrolling — and watch your clean streak and money saved grow, one Freeday at a time.

## Target User

The enormous population trying to quit or cut back on something — smokers, vapers, people cutting sugar or alcohol, people breaking a doomscrolling habit — a mass-market need spanning age and background.

## Painful Moment Solved

Wanting to quit something and having only single-purpose apps that don't talk to each other, plus feeling alone in the moment of a craving with no quick, non-judgmental tool to ride it out.

## Why It Is Different

One flexible engine works for any habit instead of a dozen single-purpose cessation apps — name your habit, log a cost-per-use, and Freeday tracks streak and running savings, with a 90-second guided 'ride the craving' moment when tempted, ending in a shareable milestone card ('47 days, $310 saved') tuned for social sharing rather than clinical tracking.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers one habit, streak tracking, and milestone cards; Freeday+ unlocks unlimited simultaneous habits, the full craving-support toolkit, and a Lock Screen craving-timer Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr — positioned as cheaper than one week of the habit itself, stated explicitly in the paywall copy.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData store for habits, relapse/reset events, and craving-session logs; savings math computed on-device; all guided craving-support content bundled locally, no network dependency.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows CloudKit sync later; an opt-in anonymous community layer is a plausible v2 but explicitly out of scope for v1 to avoid backend/moderation overhead.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None; explicitly not using LLM chat-based coaching — the craving-support flow is a fixed, well-designed guided sequence that must work instantly and offline.

## Design Direction

Clean, encouraging, and light — soft mint-to-sky-blue gradient system, rounded friendly iconography, a sunrise/counter motif for 'days free,' deliberately non-clinical, non-shaming tone.

## App Store Positioning

One app for quitting anything — not just cigarettes. Track your streak, watch your savings grow, and get through the next craving without white-knuckling it alone.

## Invalidation Criteria

If most users who log a relapse never open the app again within a week, the reset/relapse flow is driving shame-based abandonment and needs a redesign before any other feature work.

## v1 Build Scope

Habit setup with cost-per-use, daily streak tracking with non-punitive reset, craving-timer guided flow, milestone recap card + ShareLink, widget, Freeday+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- set up a habit with cost-per-use
- daily check-in / streak tracking with one-tap non-punitive relapse-and-restart
- in-the-moment craving timer with guided ride-it-out flow
- milestone streak + savings recap card
- manage multiple concurrent habits (paid tier)

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for an active craving-timer session
- WidgetKit showing current streak + savings
- ShareLink for milestone card
- haptics for streak-day and craving-timer completion

## Key Risks

- Relapse handling is the make-or-break UX moment — punitive 'streak broken, back to zero' framing will cause uninstalls; needs a genuinely non-shaming reset flow with a preserved lifetime-total stat from day one
- This category brushes against health claims — copy must stay in the 'personal tracking tool' lane, not present as medical/addiction treatment advice

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design an encouraging habit-quitting app called Freeday: soft mint-to-sky-blue gradient system, rounded friendly iconography, a sunrise/counter motif representing 'days free,' warm and non-judgmental tone throughout, avoid clinical white/red or guilt-inducing imagery, a milestone streak+savings card as the hero shareable artifact, calm and supportive rather than gamified-aggressive.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce a complete design handoff: screen-by-screen specs, interaction states, motion notes, design tokens, accessibility expectations, and a professional Claude Design prompt or import plan if the run is configured to pause for external design.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Coming at this as the person who actually has to wire up the navigation graph and state containers, not just describe screens.

The thing I want to nail down first, because nobody upstream has said it explicitly: this app has almost no navigation stack. It's fundamentally one screen (Home) with a small number of full-screen covers and sheets layered on top, and I think that should be built literally that way — a single `NavigationStack` rooted at Home (used only for the Habit Detail/Edit push and the paid-tier Multi-Habit List push), with everything else — Craving Timer, Milestone Card, Paywall, Onboarding — presented as `.fullScreenCover` or `.sheet` modifiers driven by enum-based state on a root view model, not by route objects or a router library. Given "no third-party package unless it materially reduces risk," a home-grown coordinator enum is the right call here: `enum Presentation { case none, onboarding, cravingTimer(Habit), milestoneCard(Habit), paywall }` on an `@Observable AppState`. This matters because the acute-craving flow is the whole product — if craving-timer presentation is buried in a navigation push with its own back-stack semantics, a swipe-back gesture becomes an accidental "abandon craving session" trigger, which is exactly the kind of second-order bug that undermines the reset-flow discipline everyone's been protecting. Full-screen cover with a controlled dismiss (not swipe-to-dismiss) is the only mechanism I'd allow on that screen.

State management: I want an `@Observable` `HabitStore` (or similarly named repository-facing service, matching the repository-boundary decision from per_app_product_brief) that owns a `ModelContext`, exposes computed properties for `currentStreakDays`, `lifetimeDays`, `savingsAmount: Double?`, and is the single source every view reads from — no view should run its own `@Query` against SwiftData directly for streak math, because that's how you get two screens disagreeing about "today" during a midnight rollover. Centralizing day-boundary math in one `StreakCalculator` (calendar-day-aware, injectable `Date` provider per the process note in app_features) that the store calls internally is non-negotiable; it's the thing detailed_discussion flagged as needing to live in exactly one place, and it's also the thing the widget extension and the Live Activity both need to reuse without duplicating logic — so it belongs in a shared framework target (a local Swift package or shared source group), not duplicated between the app target and the widget extension target.

On the widget/Live Activity data-sharing mechanics, which nobody has actually specified yet and which I think is a real risk if left implicit: WidgetKit and the app run in separate processes, so the widget can't just open the same SwiftData store transparently unless the app group entitlement and a shared App Group container are configured, and even then cross-process SwiftData access has real gotchas (the widget extension should do lightweight reads, not open a full ModelContainer with write access). I'd specify this concretely: an App Group (`group.com.[bundleid].freeday`) holding a small shared snapshot — current streak day count, lifetime days, savings-if-set, habit name — written by the main app every time the store mutates (reset, craving completion, habit edit), read by the widget and Live Activity. This is also what makes the "reset must synchronously invalidate the widget" requirement from detailed_discussion actually implementable: the reset write updates SwiftData AND the shared snapshot AND calls `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadAllTimelines()` in the same atomic step, so there's no window where the widget reads stale SwiftData via some lazy sync path.

On the craving timer's monotonic-time requirement: concretely, use `ContinuousClock` and store a `startInstant: ContinuousClock.Instant` plus elapsed duration, not a `Date` compared against `Date()` on a `Timer`. On resume-from-background, recompute elapsed from the stored instant rather than resuming a paused counter — this is what actually delivers the "pause-by-interruption resumes correctly" behavior design_discussion asked for, and it's worth being explicit in the handoff because it's easy to accidentally implement with `Timer.scheduledTimer` + `Date()`, which is exactly the DST/clock-change failure mode detailed_discussion warned about.

Now the actual screen-level detail, since that's what a builder needs in hand:

Home is a `VStack` on a full-bleed gradient (`LinearGradient` from mint `#A8E6CF`-ish through sky blue `#7FC7E8`-ish, roughly 135°, animated with a very slow, barely-perceptible hue drift over ~60s using `TimelineView(.animation)` rather than a static gradient — this is the "sunrise" motif doing ambient work without being a literal illustration). Content hierarchy top to bottom: small habit name label (secondary, low-emphasis, tappable to reach Habit Detail), the day-count hero number (SF Pro Rounded, black weight, ~96-120pt depending on Dynamic Type category, radial glow behind it using a `RadialGradient` mask whose opacity/blur scales with `log(streakDays)` so it's noticeably brighter at day 100 than day 1 without needing separate art assets per milestone), the savings row directly beneath in a much smaller weight (secondary color, currency-formatted, entirely absent — not hidden-but-reserving-space, actually absent from layout — when cost is unset, per the earlier decision that streak-only is equal-weight not degraded), then generous negative space, then the craving button (a large pill or circle, ~120pt diameter minimum, filled with a warmer accent color that's distinct from the mint/sky gradient so it reads as the one actionable element on a mostly-ambient screen — I'd use a soft coral/peach, not red/orange, to keep intensity without tripping the "no danger colors" rule), and at the very bottom a small text-only reset control (no background fill, no border, low-contrast gray, sized to a real 44×44pt tap target via padding even though the visible glyph is small — this is the exact spot called out as a shrink-temptation risk).

Craving Timer is a full-bleed version of the same gradient family but shifted cooler/calmer (more sky blue, less mint, slightly desaturated) with a large circular progress ring (`.trim(from:to:)` on a `Circle`, driven by elapsed/duration ratio from the `ContinuousClock` state, not a percentage recomputed from `Date`), a large monospaced-digit countdown number in the ring's center so digits don't jitter the layout as they change width, and the scripted guidance text beneath it in a card that cross-fades between script beats — no swipe/tap-to-advance controls, the script paces itself. A single small "I'm okay now" or equivalent exit-early text control, low-emphasis, top corner, that logs the session as abandoned honestly rather than completed — this needs to exist per detailed_discussion's abandoned-state requirement, and I don't think it was explicitly placed on a screen anywhere yet, so I'm putting it here.

Milestone Card is built as its own standalone SwiftUI view sized for export (I'd target a fixed 1080×1350 or similar portrait canvas so it looks intentional on Instagram/iMessage, not just a screenshot of the app), rendered to `UIImage` via `ImageRenderer` and handed to `ShareLink` as an `Image` plus a short text string — this needs to be a genuinely separate view hierarchy from Home's live view, not a screenshot of Home, because Home has UI chrome (reset control, safe-area padding) that shouldn't appear on the shareable artifact. Card content: habit-quitting framing text small at top ("Freeday — [habit name]"), day count enormous and centered, savings amount beneath if set, a thin sunrise-glow motif consistent with Home's visual language, and small Freeday wordmark/logo bottom corner since this card is the entire viral loop and needs to be recognizable when reshared without context.

Design tokens, concretely: primary gradient stops mint `#A8E6CF` → sky `#7FC7E8`; accent/craving-button coral `#FF9E80` (warm but not alert-red); neutral background for Setup/Settings `#F7F9FA` light / `#111417` dark; primary text `#1C2B2A` light / `#EAF4F2` dark; secondary text at 60% opacity of primary; corner radius scale 12/16/24 (small controls/cards/sheets); spacing scale 4/8/16/24/32/48; type scale using SF Pro Rounded for numerals and headlines (Rounded reinforces "friendly," per the spec's own "rounded friendly iconography" line) and standard SF Pro for body copy — hero number at a Dynamic-Type-relative largeTitle-and-a-half equivalent that's allowed to shrink under `.minimumScaleFactor(0.5)` rather than wrap, per the accessibility note already locked.

Component inventory a builder actually implements as reusable views: `GradientBackground(variant: .warm/.cool)`, `DayCounterHero(days: Int, glowIntensity: Double)`, `SavingsRow(amount: Double?)` (returns `EmptyView` internals when nil, not a hidden view), `CravingButton`, `QuietResetControl`, `UndoSnackbar(message:, onUndo:)`, `CravingProgressRing`, `MilestoneCardView` (the exportable one), `HabitSetupCard` with a disclosure sub-section for cost/frequency, `PaywallFeatureRow`, `SettingsDisclaimerBlock`. Keeping these as named, isolated views is what makes the pixel-parity acceptance test for reset (screenshot before/after must be indistinguishable except the number) actually verifiable — if Home is one monolithic body, it's harder to guarantee nothing else in the tree shifts.

Motion notes beyond what design_discussion already set: the day-count transition on reset should use a `.contentTransition(.numericText())` cross-fade (iOS 16+, perfect fit here) rather than a custom animation, so it reads as "the number updated" not "the screen changed." Craving-button's transient completion reinforcement is a `.scaleEffect` pulse (1.0→1.06→1.0, ~400ms, spring) plus a one-shot color highlight that fades over 800ms — cheap, no new asset. Snackbar slides from bottom with a standard spring, no bounce overshoot (overshoot on an undo affordance reads as playful/gamified, wrong tone here).

Accessibility, consolidating and adding the couple of things that were implied but not stated: Dynamic Type testing specifically at `.accessibility3` and above on Home, where I'd have the hero number and craving button both individually respect `.minimumScaleFactor` rather than the whole VStack scaling, so the craving button never shrinks below 44pt even if the number above it does. VoiceOver reading order on Home should be habit name → day count → savings (if present) → craving button → reset, with reset needing an explicit accessibility label ("Start over — today becomes day one") since a bare "Reset" label with no `accessibilityHint` under-communicates that it's a no-confirmation destructive-adjacent action, which is a real VoiceOver-user usability gap even though the sighted UI intentionally downplays it. Color contrast: verify the coral craving-button text/icon against its own fill hits WCAG AA, and verify the radial glow behind the hero number never drops the number's own contrast against the gradient background at high Dynamic Type sizes when the number is large enough to sit closer to the gradient's edge tones.

On whether this needs external Claude Design before implementation: I don't think it does, and I'd make that the explicit call rather than default to pausing. Three phases of design discussion have already produced a screen inventory, a real interaction state machine for the one interaction that matters most, explicit token values, and named reusable components — a SwiftUI builder has everything needed to start scaffolding today, and routing this through an external design tool adds a round-trip without adding decisions that are still open. The one place I'd keep a lightweight external-design option on the table, optionally, not as a blocker: the Milestone Card's exact typographic/illustrative polish, since that single artifact is the entire viral mechanism and is worth an extra visual pass if time allows after the core app is working. If that's pursued, the prompt to hand to Claude Design would be: "Design a shareable milestone card for a habit-quitting app called Freeday, portrait aspect ratio for social sharing, soft mint-to-sky-blue gradient background, a huge centered day-count number with a subtle warm radial glow behind it, an optional savings-amount line beneath in a smaller weight, small wordmark bottom corner, rounded friendly type, calm and proud tone rather than gamified or trophy-like, must look complete and intentional even when the savings line is absent." But I'd treat that as a nice-to-have polish pass on one artifact, not a gate on starting the SwiftUI build.

The biggest risk in my own plan: putting the streak/day-boundary math and the widget-snapshot-writing logic in a shared package is the architecturally correct move, but it's also the one piece of infrastructure that, if rushed, could get built as "just call the same function from both targets" without actually solving the cross-process data access problem — and if that happens, the widget will silently show stale or wrong numbers in a way that's hard to catch without literally installing the widget on a home screen and testing it, not just running the simulator. I'd flag that as the one part of this handoff that needs a deliberate build-order slot rather than being assumed to fall out for free from "good architecture."

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

It also makes an explicit call on the one open process question — no external Claude Design needed to start the build, with a narrowly-scoped optional prompt offered only for the milestone card's polish pass later. Nothing here contradicts prompt_contract, detailed_discussion, app_features, or design_discussion; it's a direct, faithful translation of those decisions into implementation-ready specifics. ## Final Output

**Screen-by-screen design specification** (extending design_discussion's inventory with implementation-level detail):

- **Home** — root of the single `NavigationStack`. Full-bleed `LinearGradient` (mint→sky, ~135°) with a very slow (~60s) hue drift via `TimelineView(.animation)`. Top-to-bottom: secondary habit-name label (tappable → Habit Detail push) · day-count hero number (SF Pro Rounded black, ~96–120pt scaled by Dynamic Type, `.minimumScaleFactor` rather than wrap) with a radial glow behind it whose intensity scales with `log(streakDays)` · savings row beneath in smaller secondary weight, *fully absent from layout* (not hidden) when cost is unset · generous negative space · craving button (coral `#FF9E80` pill/circle, ≥120pt) as the one high-saturation actionable element · quiet text-only reset control at the bottom, no fill/border, low-contrast gray, real 44×44pt tap target via padding around a visually small glyph.
- **Craving Timer** — presented via `.fullScreenCover`, no chrome, no swipe-to-dismiss (only a small explicit "I'm okay now" exit-early control, top corner, that logs the session as `abandoned`). Cooler/more-desaturated gradient variant, circular progress ring driven by `ContinuousClock` elapsed/duration ratio, monospaced-digit countdown centered in the ring, guidance script cross-fading beneath with no manual advance controls.
- **Craving Complete** — not a screen; auto-dismiss back to Home ~0.5s after completion with haptic + a one-shot `.scaleEffect` pulse/color highlight on the craving button.
- **Milestone Card / Share Sheet** — a standalone exportable view (not a Home screenshot), fixed portrait canvas (~1080×1350), rendered via `ImageRenderer`, handed to `ShareLink`. Habit-name/wordmark small at top, day count enormous and centered, savings beneath if set, thin sunrise-glow motif, small wordmark bottom corner so reshares are recognizable out of context.

**Claude Design decision**: Not needed to start the SwiftUI build — four phases of design work already provide a complete screen inventory, a specified interaction state machine, concrete tokens, and named components. Optional, non-blocking exception: an external polish pass on the Milestone Card only, since it's the entire viral artifact. If pursued, prompt: *"Design a shareable milestone card for a habit-quitting app called Freeday, portrait aspect ratio for social sharing, soft mint-to-sky-blue gradient background, a huge centered day-count number with a subtle warm radial glow behind it, an optional savings-amount line beneath in a smaller weight, small wordmark bottom corner, rounded friendly type, calm and proud tone rather than gamified or trophy-like, must look complete and intentional even when the savings line is absent."*

title: Ios Architecture Review
output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Only one voice showed up again (Codex still in cooldown), but this pass is doing real closing work rather than repeating design_handoff — it's looking specifically for the ways this app could break in a real App Store review or on a real device, and it closes several gaps nobody had nailed down yet: a hard rule that the widget/Live Activity extension gets zero SwiftData import and only reads a flat Codable snapshot (closing the "someone shares the ModelContainer across targets" shortcut that would otherwise silently produce a stale/corrupt widget), a main-actor-only write path so the snapshot write and the widget reload can't race, an explicit call to version the SwiftData schema from day one instead of retrofitting migration after real user streak data exists, a two-mechanism fix for the abandoned-craving-session gap (scenePhase hook for polite backgrounding, plus a launch-time sweep for true force-quits, since a hook can't run if the process is just gone), an explicit Live Activity `.end()` call tied to the same write path so a session never lingers on someone's lock screen, a concrete contact-picker-over-direct-fetch decision that avoids adding an unnecessary permissions prompt to a should-have feature, and a named, scoped exception to the zero-dependency policy (a test-target-only snapshot-testing library, with an explicit dependency-free fallback if that's rejected) so the single most invalidation-critical interaction — the reset pixel-parity check — is actually an automated regression test instead of a one-time manual eyeball. All six required outputs (SwiftUI architecture, framework choices, persistence plan, permissions/privacy plan, dependency/license decision, testing implications) are present and concrete, and nothing here contradicts prompt_contract, detailed_discussion, or design_handoff — it's a faithful tightening of what was already locked. No open disagreement to hash out, so calling it here rather than spinning a redundant round.

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture recommendation**: Single `NavigationStack` rooted at Home (per design_handoff) with everything else presented via enum-driven `.fullScreenCover`/`.sheet`. `HabitStore` (`@Observable`, owns the `ModelContext`) is strictly main-actor — no background-queue writes, ever — so every craving/reset write, the App Group snapshot write, and `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadAllTimelines()` happen synchronously in the same call, eliminating any race where a widget could reload before the new value lands. Craving session lifecycle gets a real two-mechanism abandonment guarantee: a `scenePhase`-triggered write on backgrounding/inactive for the polite case, plus a defensive launch-time sweep that finds any session still marked `.started` past its expected duration + grace window and retroactively marks it `abandoned` — covering true force-quits, which a scenePhase hook can never catch since the process is simply gone.

**Apple framework choices**: SwiftData for all local persistence, shipped with a `VersionedSchema`/`SchemaMigrationPlan` from v1 (not a bare unversioned `@Model`), since a 1.1 field addition without a migration story either breaks or wipes existing streak data. `ContinuousClock`/`Instant` for the craving timer (never `Date` vs. `Timer`). `UINotificationFeedbackGenerator`/`UIImpactFeedbackGenerator` for haptics — no `CHHapticEngine` custom patterns, since the spec only needs two simple taps and custom patterns add capability-check surface for no user-facing gain. `CNContactPickerViewController` (not direct `CNContactStore` fetch) for the "text someone" flow — a system-owned out-of-process picker that needs zero permission prompt, versus a disproportionate `NSContactsUsageDescription` ask for a should-have feature. StoreKit 2 with a real `.storekit` config wired into the run scheme, one "Freeday+" subscription group holding both products, mandatory Restore Purchases button, and gating routed through an `EntitlementStore` abstraction (not scattered `Transaction.currentEntitlements` calls) so paywall gating is actually unit-testable. `ActivityKit` Live Activity explicitly `.end()`ed in the same code path as session completion/abandonment — never left to expire on its own.

**Persistence and local data plan**: SwiftData is sole source of truth, versioned schema from day one. Widget/Live Activity never touch SwiftData directly — they read a small flat Codable snapshot (streak day count, lifetime days, savings-if-set, habit name) written to an App Group container, updated atomically alongside every mutation. Test target uses an isolated in-memory `ModelContainer` selected via launch argument, so tests don't pollute each other or depend on leftover simulator state.

**Permissions/privacy plan**: Near-empty Info.plist privacy surface — no location, camera, microphone, HealthKit, or photo library usage descriptions. The one place permission creep could sneak in (contacts, via the "text someone" flow) is closed by using the system contact picker instead of a direct fetch API, avoiding a permission prompt entirely for that feature.

Either way, this must be an automated test, not a one-time manual QA step.

**Testing implications**: `StreakCalculator` needs a full unit suite over the already-named edge cases (midnight-boundary setup, DST, timezone travel, leap years, reset-to-day-1), enabled specifically by the injectable date-provider seam — if that seam gets skipped under time pressure, the entire bug category tied to the invalidation criteria ships untested. Savings math needs unit tests over all four cost-set/unset × frequency-set/unset combinations, confirming the streak-only degrade path never fabricates a number from a guessed frequency. Craving timer needs a fake-`ContinuousClock`-driven test simulating backgrounding and force-quit-then-relaunch, verifying the session lands on `abandoned` rather than `completed` or stuck. Named build-verification gate before this app is called done: someone must actually install the widget on a device, perform a reset, and confirm the home-screen widget updates within one reload cycle — this is invisible in the simulator and was already flagged as the single most likely thing to get silently skipped under time pressure.
